case_name: The New York Times Company v. Microsoft Corp. and OpenAI
description: The NYT sued OpenAI and Microsoft alleging that millions of its articles were used without permission to train GPT models. The complaint included examples of ChatGPT reproducing NYT articles nearly verbatim, and seeks billions in damages.

Getty sued Stability AI for copying over 12 million photographs and their metadata — including Getty watermarks — to train Stable Diffusion without a license or compensation. The generated images sometimes include distorted Getty watermarks.

case_name: Andersen v. Stability AI, Midjourney, and DeviantArt
plaintiff: Sarah Andersen, Kelly McKernan, Karla Ortiz (class action)
description: Three visual artists filed a class action lawsuit alleging their copyrighted artwork was scraped and used to train AI image generators without consent, compensation, or credit. The case established key legal arguments about AI training data as copyright infringement.

case_name: Authors Guild et al. v. OpenAI
plaintiff: Authors Guild (John Grisham, Jodi Picoult, George R.R. Martin, and 14 others)
description: Seventeen major authors sued OpenAI claiming their books were used without permission to train ChatGPT. The Authors Guild argued this constitutes systematic copyright infringement on a massive scale.

plaintiff: Sarah Silverman, Christopher Golden, Richard Kadrey
description: Comedian and author Sarah Silverman, along with two other authors, sued OpenAI and Meta separately, alleging their books were used without authorization in AI training datasets scraped from piracy sites.

plaintiff: Sarah Silverman, Christopher Golden, Richard Kadrey
description: Companion case to Silverman v. OpenAI — same plaintiffs sued Meta for using their books to train LLaMA without permission.
Two progressive news outlets sued OpenAI for stripping copyright management information from their articles when scraping them to train ChatGPT — violating the Digital Millennium Copyright Act.

Major music publishers sued Anthropic for training Claude on copyrighted song lyrics without authorization. The complaint showed Claude reproducing verbatim lyrics from Beatles and Rolling Stones songs.

case_name: Universal Music Group v. Suno / Udio
plaintiff: Universal Music Group, Sony Music, Warner Music Group (RIAA)
court: U.S. District Court, D. Mass. / S.D.N.Y.
description: The major record labels, through the RIAA, sued two AI music generators — Suno and Udio — for training on copyrighted recordings without permission. The complaints seek up to $150,000 per infringed work.

Thomson Reuters sued legal AI startup ROSS Intelligence for using Westlaw content to train its AI legal research tool. In February 2024, the court ruled in Thomson Reuters's favor — the first major AI training copyright case to reach a verdict.

case_name: Doe v. GitHub, OpenAI, and Microsoft
description: A class action by developers alleging that GitHub Copilot was trained on public code repositories without proper attribution, violating open source licenses and copyright law. Seeks $9 billion in damages.

plaintiff: Michael Chabon, Ta-Nehisi Coates, Junot Díaz, and others
description: Distinguished authors including Pulitzer Prize winners sued Meta for using their books — obtained from piracy sites like Bibliotik — to train LLaMA models without permission or compensation.

New York's AG launched an investigation into Character.AI after a Florida teenager's suicide was linked to conversations with a chatbot. The investigation focuses on whether the platform violated consumer protection laws by targeting minors.

The mother of a 14-year-old boy who died by suicide sued Character.AI and Google, alleging the AI chatbot encouraged her son's suicidal ideation and that Character.AI designed addictive features targeting minors. The case is the first wrongful death lawsuit against an AI company.

The FTC took action against AI writing tool Rytr for enabling users to generate fake reviews at scale, a practice the FTC characterized as an unfair or deceptive act. Rytr agreed to stop selling any service that generates consumer reviews.
Authors sued NVIDIA for using their books in the NeMo AI training dataset without permission. The case names NVIDIA as a defendant in AI training copyright disputes alongside the model developers.

case_name: Intercept Media v. OpenAI and Microsoft
plaintiff: The Intercept / In These Times / Raw Story
description: Independent news publications sued OpenAI and Microsoft for removing copyright management information from their journalism when scraping it for AI training, violating the DMCA.

plaintiff: Mike Huckabee and other Christian authors
description: Former Arkansas governor and pastor Mike Huckabee joined other Christian authors in a copyright lawsuit against Meta for allegedly using their books from piracy sites to train LLaMA.
